nativeRange.habitat: Supralittoral splash pools and high-shore rocky-intertidal tide pools along the Pacific coast of North America, from central Baja California north to coastal Alaska. These pools are isolated from ocean moderation and experience extreme swings in temperature, salinity, oxygen, and pH between rain, evaporation, and wave-splash events.

decorPreferences: bare-bottom culture container or jar (4-20 L)
gentle aeration via airstone (large coarse bubbles, not fine)
rubble rock, a Chaetomorpha clump, or a sponge surface for biofilm grazing and egg-laying
ambient or moderate refugium-style lighting (no direct strong reef lighting required)
no copper or metal surfaces in contact with the culture water
in display use, seed into a plumbed refugium with macroalgae and rubble where pods can reproduce shielded from predators

diet.notes: Has been documented grazing on live single-celled microalgae (phytoplankton), bacterial biofilms, and fine organic detritus. Reported reliable culture foods include live Nannochloropsis, Tetraselmis, Isochrysis galbana, and diatom blends (e.g. OceanMagik, Phyto-Feast, Reef Nutrition phyto). Powdered spirulina is sometimes used as a supplement but generally advised only in trace amounts to avoid fouling. A common dosing guide is roughly 5-10 mL of concentrated phyto per ~75 L of culture water every other day, adjusted to keep the water faintly green between feedings.

goodWith: monoculture container (no predators) for active culturing
plumbed refugiums with macroalgae (Chaetomorpha) and rubble for sheltered reproduction
as live food for mandarins, scooter blennies, and other dragonets
as live food for seahorses, pipefish, and other syngnathids
as live food for anthias, fairy and flasher wrasses, and other planktivores
as a coral feed for NPS corals (Tubastraea / sun corals, Dendronephthya, gorgonians)
as a polyp feed for many LPS corals and for filter-feeding clams, sponges, and feather dusters
avoidWith: copper or copper-based medications anywhere in the system
predator-heavy display tanks with no refugium (population will be eaten faster than it reproduces)
fine-pore mechanical filtration without a sponge cover on intakes (will strip pods and nauplii)
protein skimmers running during initial seeding (turn off for 1-2 hours after dosing)
very large planktivorous fish that strip pods out of the rockwork before slower target feeders can reach them

summary: The Tigger Pod (Tigriopus californicus) is a tiny red-orange harpacticoid copepod native to Pacific splash pools, cultured widely as a live food and refugium seed for reef aquariums. It is widely valued for feeding picky planktivores such as mandarins, dragonets, pipefish, and leopard wrasses.
careNotes: Tigriopus californicus is kept almost exclusively as a culture and live-food organism rather than a display species; the minimum tank size listed reflects the smallest practical culture container — a 4 L jar up to a 20 L tub — rather than a display aquarium. Adults are roughly 1-2 mm long, bright red-orange from astaxanthin pigment (the same carotenoid that colors salmon and shrimp), and swim in a distinctive jerky stop-and-go pattern that is highly stimulating to visual planktivores. Generation time is generally reported at around 3-4 weeks, with new broods every 8-12 days and females producing multiple clutches over a lifespan that has been reported up to roughly 6 months under laboratory conditions. Cultures are typically maintained in small bare-bottom containers, fed live phytoplankton (such as Nannochloropsis, Tetraselmis, Isochrysis, or a commercial phyto blend) and gently aerated with coarse bubbles rather than fine mist. In the wild, this species has been reported to tolerate an extreme range of conditions — near-freshwater to above 100 ppt hypersaline, and pool temperatures occasionally exceeding 38-40 C — but stable reproduction in culture is generally achieved at reef-tank salinity (SG 1.018-1.026) and 18-26 C. The idea that Tigriopus californicus is a cold-water species requiring chilled tanks is widely considered a misconception; populations have been cultured year-round in warm greenhouses without issue. Adults have been reported to cannibalize nauplii (the youngest larval stage), so regularly harvesting from the water column or providing a refugium with shelter pockets is generally advised to protect younger stages. For seeding a refugium, the standard practice is to dim or turn off display lighting, briefly stop pumps and the protein skimmer, and pour the culture directly into the refugium rockwork or macroalgae at night so pods can settle without being immediately stripped by predators or filtration.
breedingNotes: Reproduces sexually with internal fertilization. Mature females have been reported to carry a single egg sac of a few dozen eggs at a time, releasing a new brood approximately every 8-12 days under favorable conditions, and producing multiple successive clutches over their lifetime. Generation time from nauplius to reproductive adult is roughly 3-4 weeks at typical reef temperatures, slower than Tisbe biminiensis or Apocyclops panamensis but faster than many other harpacticoids. Nauplii — the microscopic first larval stages — are about 70-150 microns and drift freely in the water column. Intermediate copepodid stages begin crawling on surfaces, while adults are semi-benthic and tend to be found near the water surface. Cultures are commonly maintained by feeding live phytoplankton daily or every other day, providing rubble or a sponge for biofilm grazing, and harvesting either via sieve (~100 micron mesh) or by siphoning out the upper water column where adults concentrate. Backup cultures are widely recommended in case a primary culture crashes. Population density in healthy cultures has been reported to reach hundreds to thousands of copepods per liter.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Designated primary; LiveAquaria does not currently carry a Tigger Pods / Tigriopus californicus product page (search returned only unrelated fish results and direct product URL 404'd). Substituted AlgaeBarn Tig Pods product page as the functional primary source. URL retained for reviewer reference.

additional.name: Reef2Reef Wiki - Tigger-Pods (Tigriopus californicus): addressing the cold water myth
additional.notes: Designated secondary; the specific Reef2Reef article URL returned HTTP 403 Forbidden during this research run. Substituted AlgaeBarn, Reef Nutrition, Pod Your Reef, and Wikipedia as functional cross-sources. URL retained for reviewer reference.

additional.name: AlgaeBarn - Tig Pods (Tigriopus californicus)
additional.notes: Functional primary substitute. Source for color (large red), splash-pool habitat tolerance (acidity, hypoxia, extreme temperatures), females laying hundreds of eggs, refugium and display seeding for mandarins/seahorses/anthias/wrasses/SPS/LPS/NPS corals/clams/filter feeders, recommended phyto feed (OceanMagik), and 3000+ pods per 16 oz bottle (mixed nauplii through adult sizes).

additional.name: Reef Nutrition - Tigger-Pods (Tigriopus californicus)
additional.notes: Confirmed trade-name origin (Reef Nutrition owns the 'Tigger-Pods' brand). Confirmed size class above 300 microns at packaging, primary diet of microalgae (Phyto-Feast), warm-water tolerance (warmer water increases metabolism and reproduction rate; pods do well in cool water too), refugium-thrives-without-predators guidance, hides in rockwork and macroalgae, and refrigerator storage up to ~2 weeks with cap popped open.

additional.name: Pod Your Reef - Tigriopus californicus Copepods: A Scientific and Practical Guide for Reef Aquarists
additional.notes: Triangulation source for adult size 1-2 mm (up to 2.5-3 mm max), nauplii 70-150 microns, generation time ~1 month, brood cycle 8-12 days, optimal salinity SG 1.024-1.026 (tolerance 1.015-1.030+), euryhaline range to >100 ppt, optimal temperature low 60s to 80s F, diet (Isochrysis/Nannochloropsis/Tetraselmis/Chaetoceros/diatoms), refugium seeding protocol (lights off, pumps off 1-2 h, skimmer off, distribute into rockwork), copper kills pods, comparison to Tisbe and Apocyclops, nutritional profile (40-55% protein, high DHA/EPA, astaxanthin). Resolved cold-water-species myth in favor of broad-temperature culture.

additional.name: Wikipedia - Tigriopus californicus
additional.notes: Taxonomy reference (family Harpacticidae, order Harpacticoida, class Copepoda). Native range central Baja California, Mexico to Alaska, USA. Habitat upper intertidal splash pools. Lifespan reported up to 6 months in laboratory conditions. Population densities ~800 copepods/L typical with dense pools reaching 20,000/L. Temperature extremes in habitat occasionally exceed 40 C.

crustacean.moltingNotes: As copepods, Tigriopus californicus molts repeatedly through six naupliar (N1-N6) and five copepodid (C1-C5) stages before reaching the adult (C6) stage. Each molt is closely spaced - generally on the order of every 1-3 days under warm, well-fed conditions - so the entire larval-to-adult sequence is typically completed in roughly 2-3 weeks. Shed exuviae are microscopic and contribute negligibly to the culture's particulate load. The reported 3-day value is a typical inter-stage interval; the cumulative development time from nauplius to reproductive adult is generally reported at around 3-4 weeks.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Sexual reproduction with internal fertilization; females carry a single egg sac with a few dozen eggs per brood and release multiple successive broods. See top-level breedingNotes for full culture protocol.
